map.subtitle: Big Idea 1: Creative Development   •   1 day   •   LO CRD-1.A, CRD-1.B, CRD-1.C
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, share-out cues, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Collaboration Post-Mortem and Exercise 2: The Collaboration Clinic (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Better Together? (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing — all inside the single teach day)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 7 questions across LO CRD-1.A, CRD-1.B, and CRD-1.C, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (one teach day: what counts as a computing innovation, why collaboration and diverse perspectives improve it, and how real teams work — online tools, pair programming, interpersonal skills).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Scheduling: Topic 1.1 is a 1-day topic and the opening content lesson of the course. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a second day. Topic 1.2 (Program Function and Purpose) begins immediately after the quiz.

exercise1.title: Collaboration Post-Mortem
exercise1.intro: You are the consultant hired to explain why CampusNight — a student-built event-planning app — flopped on launch night. The team worked hard, wrote plenty of code, and still shipped an app that locked out part of the school. Your evidence is the team's own project log. For each entry you will name the collaboration principle that was violated, diagnose what it did to the product, and prescribe what should have happened instead. Part A works the log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives CRD-1.A, CRD-1.B, and CRD-1.C.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Project Log: Five Entries from the CampusNight Team
exercise1.narrative: The team's advisor exported five entries from the project's chat history and planning documents. Your job is the post-mortem: read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: name the collaboration principle at stake, diagnose what the entry did to the product, then prescribe what the team should have done instead.

rows: Kickoff decision
Four friends from the same coding club decided 'we already know what students want' and skipped the planned user survey
All four developers — same grade, same club, same phones
Nobody outside the team was ever asked a single question
Work split
The app was split into four parts; members agreed never to touch or comment on each other's parts
Each member, working alone
The four parts were first combined the night before launch
File sharing
Code traveled by emailed zip attachments; two members spent a weekend rebuilding work after overwriting each other's versions
The two back-end members
No shared workspace, version history, or comment thread existed
'Pair programming' session
One member typed for two hours while the partner watched silently, 'so as not to interrupt'
The two front-end members
The silent partner spotted a login bug but never mentioned it
The sign-up argument
Two members disagreed about the RSVP flow; the louder one refused to budge until the other gave up and quietly stopped contributing
The whole team heard it
Launch night: students without data plans couldn't RSVP at all — the quiet member had raised exactly that concern

exercise1.scenarioNote: Log entries adapted from the Day 1 bell ringer and the group-work-vs-collaboration comparison; terminology and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which single log entry did the most damage to the final product, and why that one?

partA.q: Before assigning blame, establish what was being built. Apply the CED's test: is CampusNight a computing innovation at all? State the test, rule on the app, classify it as physical, nonphysical software, or a nonphysical concept — and give one example of each of the other two types.
partA.answer: Yes. The test (EK CRD-1.A.1): a computing innovation includes a program as an integral part of its function — CampusNight IS a program, so it passes immediately. By type (EK CRD-1.A.2) it is nonphysical computing software, like the CED's picture-editing-software example. The other two shapes: physical (e.g., a self-driving car — hardware whose function depends on an integral program) and a nonphysical computing concept (e.g., e-commerce — an idea that exists only because programs enable it). Full credit requires the test stated, the ruling, the correct type, and a valid example of each of the other two shapes.

partA.q: Entry 1 and the launch-night failure in Entry 5's detail column are connected. Explain how four similar developers skipping the user survey produced an app that locked out students without data plans — name the CED concept that describes this failure, and state what information gathered from potential users would have changed.
partA.answer: The failure is BIAS. All four developers share one perspective — same club, same grade, same phones, presumably all with data plans — and collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid bias in development (EK CRD-1.A.4); this team had no diversity of perspective to catch the assumption 'everyone has mobile data.' Skipping the survey removed the other safeguard: consultation and communication with users are important aspects of development (EK CRD-1.A.5). Information gathered from potential users — including students without data plans — would have let the team understand the app's purpose from diverse perspectives and develop a program that fully incorporates them (EK CRD-1.A.6): an offline or web-based RSVP path would have surfaced as a requirement before any code was written. Full credit names bias, cites the missing consultation, and states a concrete design change the user information would have driven.

partA.q: Entries 3 and 4 are both failures of collaboration machinery. For each entry: name what the CED says should have happened, and identify the exact moment the correct practice would have saved.
partA.answer: Entry 3: online tools support collaboration by allowing programmers to SHARE and PROVIDE FEEDBACK on ideas and documents (EK CRD-1.B.1). Emailed zips share without feedback, history, or a current version — a shared online workspace with version tracking would have prevented the overwrite weekend entirely, since both members would have been editing one tracked copy with visible changes. Entry 4: pair programming is a common model that exists to facilitate collaboration (EK CRD-1.B.2), but the model requires an active navigator — one programmer types while the other reviews each line and speaks up, with roles rotating. A silent watcher is not pair programming; the moment the real model would have saved is the spotted-but-unmentioned login bug, which an engaged navigator flags the second it appears. Full credit treats the two entries separately and names both the EK idea and the saved moment.

partA.q: Entries 2 and 5 together explain what the finished product reflects. Using EK CRD-1.A.3, state what an effectively collaborative product would have reflected — then name the interpersonal skills missing in Entry 5, and describe what practicing ONE of them would have looked like.
partA.answer: Effective collaboration produces a computing innovation that reflects the diversity of talents and perspectives of those who designed it (EK CRD-1.A.3). CampusNight reflects the opposite: four sealed compartments (Entry 2) minus one silenced voice (Entry 5). The interpersonal skills missing in Entry 5 come straight from EK CRD-1.C.1 — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation; the argument was settled by volume and endurance instead. Practicing one concretely: conflict resolution — treat the RSVP disagreement as a design question, lay out both flows' assumptions, and test them against user information rather than loudness; the quiet member's data-plan concern would then have decided the argument on evidence, since it was exactly right. (Any one skill, described as a concrete move, earns the point.)

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: A classmate reviews the same log and concludes: 'The real problem was talent — four stronger programmers would have shipped a great app.' Argue the CED's counterpoint: what do four even-stronger copies of the SAME perspective still fail to produce?
partB.answer: Skill was not the bottleneck — perspective was. Four stronger programmers with the same background still produce an innovation that reflects one perspective rather than the diversity of talents and perspectives effective collaboration provides (EK CRD-1.A.3), and they still share the same blind spots, so the bias that locked out students without data plans survives intact (EK CRD-1.A.4). Better code implementing a biased design is just a more polished failure. The fix is diversity of input — collaborators and consulted users outside the team's demographic — not more of the same input.

partB.q: The team gets one semester to build CampusNight 2.0. Design their user-consultation plan: whom should they gather information from, when, and what does the CED say that information is actually used for?
partB.answer: Gather information from POTENTIAL users, not just current fans (EK CRD-1.A.6): students without smartphones or data plans, students who never attend events, the staff who approve them, and the club leaders who post them — the people version 1 designed around. Timing matters as much as the list: consultation and communication with users are important aspects of DEVELOPMENT (EK CRD-1.A.5), so the plan needs check-ins throughout the semester — surveys before design, prototype feedback mid-build — not a single survey at the start. Per EK CRD-1.A.6, the information is used to understand the purpose of the program from diverse perspectives and to develop a program that fully incorporates those perspectives.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Open-source projects like the Python language are built by thousands of contributors who never meet. Explain how a pull request satisfies EK CRD-1.B.1's description of what online tools do — and name two EK CRD-1.C.1 interpersonal skills that appear, in written form, in a typical code-review thread.
partB.answer: A pull request is EK CRD-1.B.1 made concrete: an online tool that lets programmers SHARE (the proposed change is posted publicly for anyone to inspect) and PROVIDE FEEDBACK (reviewers comment on it line by line, and nothing merges until the discussion resolves) on ideas and documents. The interpersonal skills of EK CRD-1.C.1 run in written form: communication (the contributor explains what the change does and why; reviewers explain objections), and consensus building or negotiation (maintainers and contributors converge on a version everyone accepts, often through several rounds of revision and compromise). The scale changes from a classroom pair to thousands of strangers; the EKs do not.

exercise2.title: The Collaboration Clinic
exercise2.subtitle: 4 cases - diagnose and prescribe
exercise2.intro: You run the collaboration clinic. Four development teams arrive, each with a project in trouble. For each case: read the signs and name the collaboration principle at risk, explain what is actually going wrong and prescribe the CED-backed fix, and — for enrichment — classify the innovation being built or name the one interpersonal skill the team most needs. Vague 'work together better' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): the principle at risk + what is going wrong + the prescription, grounded in CED language, for all 4 cases. Enrichment (optional): also classify the team's innovation (physical / software / concept) or name the single most-needed interpersonal skill.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every case
method: Anchor your gut diagnosis before analyzing. Writing your own read first keeps a convincing wrong frame from swaying you.

Match the symptoms to the principle at risk: a team that all thinks alike (bias), users never consulted, tools that share without feedback, a pair-programming model gone passive, or interpersonal skills nobody is practicing.

State what the team should change and WHY the CED backs it: diverse perspectives avoid bias, user information reveals purpose, online tools exist for sharing AND feedback, pair programming needs an active navigator, and deadlocks take consensus building or negotiation. A prescription without its reason is incomplete.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (classification and skills): strong diagnoses also pin down WHAT is being built — physical innovation, nonphysical software, or nonphysical concept (EK CRD-1.A.2) — or name the single interpersonal skill (EK CRD-1.C.1) that would unlock the team: communication for the silent pair, consensus building for the deadlock, conflict resolution for the standoff, negotiation when both sides must give ground.
fields: Signs (what in the case tells you which principle is at risk):
Diagnosis + prescription (what is going wrong and the CED-backed fix):
Enrichment (innovation type, or the one interpersonal skill this team needs most):

scenarios.text: A team of four seniors — all in the same friend group, all varsity athletes with the latest phones — is building a fitness-challenge app for the whole school. They skipped interviews 'to save time,' reasoning that 'we ARE the users.' Early testers who don't play sports say the app makes them feel out of place, and students with older phones can't run it at all.

key.signs: every developer shares one perspective (same friend group, same athletic background, same hardware), and nobody outside that circle was consulted — the two classic warning signs that bias is entering the innovation (EK CRD-1.A.4, A.5). 'We ARE the users' is the giveaway sentence: the team mistook itself for the whole audience.
key.mechanism: the team's single perspective hardened into the product's assumptions — 'users are athletes with new phones' — and shipped as bias. Prescribe two moves: bring diverse perspectives into the collaboration, since diversity helps avoid bias (EK CRD-1.A.4), and gather information from POTENTIAL users — non-athletes, older-phone owners — to understand the app's purpose from their perspectives and incorporate them into the design (EK CRD-1.A.5, A.6). The sentence 'we are the users' should always trigger a survey, not replace one.
key.subtype: the fitness-challenge app is nonphysical computing software (EK CRD-1.A.2). Most-needed interpersonal skill: communication — pointed outward, toward the users the team does not resemble.

scenarios.text: Two students pair program every day: the stronger coder always drives 'to keep the speed up' while the other watches in silence. The quiet partner has privately noticed several bugs but feels pointing them out 'isn't my place.' The program grows quickly — and so does its bug list.

key.signs: one keyboard but only one active mind — a permanent driver, a silent watcher, and observations that never get spoken. Pair programming exists to facilitate collaboration (EK CRD-1.B.2), and none is actually happening; the growing bug list alongside the silent partner's private catches is the confirming evidence.
key.mechanism: the model has collapsed into solo programming with an audience: the navigator's entire job — reviewing each line in real time and speaking up — has been switched off, so bugs that were literally SEEN still ship. Prescribe restoring the model (EK CRD-1.B.2): the navigator actively reviews and questions every line, and the roles ROTATE on a fixed schedule so both perspectives shape the code and both students grow. The bug list is the receipt for every unspoken observation.
key.subtype: the missing interpersonal skill is communication (EK CRD-1.C.1). The navigator role is structured permission to speak — with rotation plus a spoken-review norm, silence becomes the rule violation, not the comment.

scenarios.text: Six students from three different schools won a grant to build a community-garden sensor kit — a soil-moisture probe whose onboard program texts volunteers when garden beds need water. They can meet in person only once a month, so they've been mailing a USB drive back and forth; whoever holds the drive owns 'the real version,' and everyone else waits.

key.signs: a distributed team bottlenecked by a physical artifact — one 'real version,' weeks of waiting, and no way to see or comment on teammates' work between meetings. This is precisely the gap online collaboration tools exist to fill (EK CRD-1.B.1).
key.mechanism: collaboration has been serialized: only the drive-holder can contribute, and feedback cannot happen at all until the monthly meeting, so five of six perspectives sit idle at any moment. Prescribe online tools that let the whole team share AND provide feedback on ideas and documents continuously (EK CRD-1.B.1): a shared repository or shared documents give everyone the current version, a change history, and a comment thread — turning six serial workers back into one collaborating team, no matter the distance.
key.subtype: the sensor kit is a PHYSICAL computing innovation — hardware with a program integral to its function, like the CED's self-driving car (EK CRD-1.A.1, A.2). Worth naming: physical innovations still live or die by software collaboration.

scenarios.text: A five-person team is designing SwapShelf, a school-wide system where students trade used books through QR codes and an online credit balance. The team is deadlocked 3–2 on the credit rules, and the majority plans to simply outvote the minority and 'move on.' The two sides now talk only through a group chat where messages keep getting misread — and nobody has asked the students or librarians who would actually run the swaps.

key.signs: a three-way pile-up — a deadlock about to be settled by force instead of agreement, communication decayed into misread group-chat messages, and zero consultation with the people who would actually use the system (EK CRD-1.C.1 and CRD-1.A.5 both flashing at once).
key.mechanism: outvoting ends the argument but not the disagreement — the outvoted minority disengages, and the product quietly loses their perspectives (EK CRD-1.A.3's warning). Prescribe the interpersonal skills by name (EK CRD-1.C.1): consensus building (find the shared goal both credit proposals serve), negotiation (each side yields on the parts it values less), and conflict resolution (move the dispute from the group chat to a live conversation, where tone survives). Then let user information arbitrate: information gathered from students and librarians reveals the system's purpose from diverse perspectives (EK CRD-1.A.5, A.6) — deadlocks usually dissolve when evidence replaces volume.
key.subtype: SwapShelf is closest to a nonphysical computing CONCEPT — like e-commerce, it is an idea (trading books via credits) that exists only because programs enable it (EK CRD-1.A.2). Most-needed interpersonal skill, if only one can be chosen: consensus building.

discussion.title: Better Together?
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening + Mid-lesson + Closing (all Day 1)
discussion.overview: Every innovation you'll study this year — and the Create Performance Task you'll build in the spring — comes down to people making something together without flattening each other's ideas. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will test whether more minds really beat one, decide what separates true collaboration from politely divided labor, and write the collaboration norms your own teams will actually be held to this year.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Compare merge counts from the bell ringer: how many needs did your partner catch that you missed? Whose lists overlapped the most — friends who know each other well, or near-strangers? What does that pattern suggest about who makes the most useful collaborator?

Question 2. Name an app or device that clearly wasn't designed with someone you know in mind — a grandparent, a younger sibling, a friend without reliable internet. What question would the design team have needed to ask, and to whom?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the group-work-vs-collaboration comparison
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The dividing line
items.text: Four names on a project does not make it a collaboration. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, state the test that separates true collaboration from divided-up group work — then check your test against the bell ringer: does the list merge pass it?

items.label: Task B - The skills ranking
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: rank the four interpersonal skills — communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, negotiation — from easiest to hardest for teams your age. Record your group's hardest one, and the moment in a typical project where it breaks down.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The team charter
items.text: This spring, teams like yours will build real programs — and every failure mode from today (one shared perspective, no user input, silent partners, deadlocks settled by volume) will be waiting. Draft three collaboration norms for a project team this year. For each norm, name the failure it prevents and the idea that backs it: diverse perspectives, user consultation, sharing with feedback, an active navigator, or one of the four interpersonal skills.
Our group's verdict (the one norm most teams will struggle to keep + one-sentence justification):
